title: Routing
subtitle: Build multi-page apps with Angular's powerful router
description: Master Angular's Router — configure routes, lazy load pages, pass parameters, build nested layouts, protect routes with guards, and navigate programmatically with full type safety.
keyPoints: provideRouter() sets up the router with your route definitions in app.config.ts
loadComponent() lazy-loads a standalone component only when its route is visited
RouterLink navigates without a full page reload — keeping Angular's state intact
Route parameters (:id) are bound directly to component inputs via withComponentInputBinding()
Child routes share a parent layout (shell pattern) — the parent renders <router-outlet />
Route guards (CanActivateFn) protect routes from unauthorised access
withViewTransitions() enables smooth CSS View Transitions between route changes

sections.title: What Is the Angular Router?
sections.content:
The Angular Router is a client-side navigation library that maps URL paths to components. When the URL changes, Angular swaps the displayed component without reloading the browser page — this is what makes Angular a Single Page Application (SPA).

The router synchronises the browser URL, the browser history (back/forward buttons), and the currently displayed component tree, all automatically.

Key pieces you will work with:
• Routes array — maps URL paths to components
• provideRouter() — registers the router in the app
• <router-outlet> — the placeholder where routed components render
• RouterLink — the directive that creates navigation links
• Router service — lets you navigate programmatically from TypeScript
• ActivatedRoute — lets a component read its own URL parameters

sections.caption: The router intercepts clicks on RouterLink, updates the URL without reload, matches the new URL to a route, runs any guards, then renders the matched component in <router-outlet>.

sections.title: Setting Up the Router
sections.content:
In a standalone Angular app (Angular 17+), you configure the router in app.config.ts by calling provideRouter() inside the providers array. This replaces the old RouterModule.forRoot() approach.

The provideRouter() function accepts your routes array and optional feature functions:
• withComponentInputBinding() — automatically binds route params/data to component inputs
• withViewTransitions() — enables smooth CSS View Transitions between routes
• withDebugTracing() — logs every router event to the console (dev only)
• withHashLocation() — uses #/path URLs instead of /path (for static hosting without URL rewriting)

sections.title: Router Setup in app.config.ts
sections.explanation: withComponentInputBinding() is the key feature that lets you receive route parameters as component inputs instead of manually injecting ActivatedRoute — much cleaner!

sections.title: Defining Routes — app.routes.ts
sections.explanation: path: '**' is the catch-all wildcard — it must always be the last route. The router checks routes from top to bottom and uses the first match.

sections.title: RouterLink — Navigation Without Page Reloads
sections.content:
RouterLink is a directive that turns any element into a navigation link. Unlike a plain <a href>, RouterLink uses the Angular router, so the page never fully reloads — Angular's state, services, and the DOM all stay intact.

Key RouterLink features:
• [routerLink]="'/path'" — absolute path
• [routerLink]="['user', userId]" — array form, segments joined automatically
• routerLinkActive — adds a CSS class when the route is active
• [routerLinkActiveOptions]="{ exact: true }" — only active on exact match (important for '/')
• [queryParams]="{ search: 'Angular' }" — adds ?search=Angular to the URL
• [fragment]="'section'" — adds #section to the URL

sections.title: RouterLink and RouterLinkActive
sections.code:
import { Component } from '@angular/core'; import { RouterLink, RouterLinkActive } from '@angular/router'; @Component({ selector: 'app-nav', standalone: true, imports: [RouterLink, RouterLinkActive], template: `
Home My Profile Search Angular Docs → Installation
  • Settings
    • `, styles: [` a.active { color: #DD0031; font-weight: 700; border-bottom: 2px solid #DD0031; } li.menu-active { background: #f1f5f9; } `] }) export class NavComponent { userId = 42; }
sections.explanation: [routerLinkActiveOptions]="{ exact: true }" is important for the root path '/'. Without it, the Home link would always be active because every URL starts with '/'.

sections.title: Lazy Loading — loadComponent()
sections.content:
Lazy loading means a component's JavaScript is only downloaded when its route is first visited. This dramatically reduces the initial bundle size and speeds up the first page load.

With standalone components, lazy loading is as simple as replacing:
  component: HomeComponent
with:
  loadComponent: () => import('./home/home.component').then(m => m.HomeComponent)

Angular's bundler (esbuild) automatically splits the HomeComponent and all its dependencies into a separate JavaScript chunk. That chunk is only fetched from the server when the user navigates to '/'.

For a group of related routes (a feature area), use loadChildren() to lazy-load an entire routes array — one network request for all routes in that feature.

sections.title: Lazy Loading with loadComponent and loadChildren
sections.explanation: loadChildren() is ideal for feature areas because related components share helper code — bundling them together avoids repeating that code in multiple chunks.

sections.title: Route Parameters and withComponentInputBinding
sections.content:
Routes can contain dynamic segments — parts of the URL that vary per navigation. These are called route parameters and are declared with a colon prefix: path: 'product/:id'.

With withComponentInputBinding() enabled in your app config, Angular automatically passes the route parameter as a component input. The input name must match the parameter name exactly.

You can receive:
• Route params  (:id)         — as a required input()
• Query params  (?search=...)  — as an optional input()
• Route data    (data: {...})  — as an optional input()

All of these become signal-based inputs when you use the input() function.

sections.title: Route Parameters as Component Inputs
sections.explanation: effect() re-runs whenever the id() signal changes — so navigating from /product/1 to /product/2 automatically triggers a fresh data load without needing ngOnChanges.

sections.title: Nested Routes and the Shell Pattern
sections.content:
Nested routes allow a parent component to provide a persistent layout (sidebar, header, tabs) while child components render inside a <router-outlet> inside that parent.

This is called the Shell Pattern — the shell component is the frame, and child routes swap in and out as the content area.

In this application, ShellComponent is the frame (header + sidebar). All pages are children of the shell route. When you navigate between pages, only the content area inside the shell's <router-outlet> changes — the sidebar and header stay mounted and don't re-render.

You can nest routes as deeply as you like. Each level needs its own <router-outlet> in its template.

sections.title: Nested Routes — Shell Pattern
sections.explanation: The key insight: ShellComponent renders ONCE and stays alive for the entire session. Only the <router-outlet> content changes. This makes transitions faster and preserves sidebar state (scroll position, open/closed items).

sections.caption: The shell renders once. Only the <router-outlet> content changes on navigation — sidebar state, scroll position, and header are preserved across all page navigations.

sections.title: Route Guards — Protecting Routes
sections.content:
Route guards are functions that run before a route is activated. They can allow or deny access, or redirect the user to another route.

The modern approach uses functional guards (CanActivateFn) — just a function, no class needed.

Common guard types:
• canActivate  — can the user enter this route?
• canDeactivate — can the user leave this route? (unsaved changes warning)
• canMatch     — should this route be considered at all? (A/B testing, feature flags)
• resolve      — pre-load data before the route activates

Guards return boolean, UrlTree (redirect), or a Promise/Observable of either.

sections.title: Functional Route Guard
sections.explanation: inject() works inside functional guards — Angular runs guards in an injection context. Returning a UrlTree instead of false triggers a redirect, which is better UX than a silent navigation block.

sections.title: Programmatic Navigation — Router.navigate()
sections.content:
Sometimes you need to navigate from TypeScript code — after a form submission, after a login, or in response to a service event. For this, inject the Router service and call navigate() or navigateByUrl().

• router.navigate(['/user', id]) — array form, recommended
• router.navigateByUrl('/user/42') — string URL, simple but less flexible
• router.navigate(['..', 'sibling'], { relativeTo: route }) — relative navigation
• { replaceUrl: true } — replaces the current history entry (login flow)
• { queryParams: {...} } — adds query string parameters
• { fragment: 'section' } — adds a hash fragment

sections.title: Programmatic Navigation
sections.explanation: { replaceUrl: true } in navigateByUrl is important for auth flows — without it, pressing the back button after login takes the user back to the login page, which is confusing.

sections.content: Always use RouterLink in templates and Router.navigate() in TypeScript — never set window.location.href directly. Using window.location causes a full browser reload, which destroys all Angular state, services, and component trees. The Angular router keeps all of this alive.

sections.title: Reading Route Data — withViewTransitions Bonus
sections.explanation: withComponentInputBinding() maps BOTH route params AND route data to inputs. This means a single component can receive its configuration entirely through its signal inputs — no ActivatedRoute injection needed in most cases.

interviewQuestions.q: How do you set up the Angular Router in a standalone app?
interviewQuestions.a: Call provideRouter(routes) inside the providers array of ApplicationConfig in app.config.ts. Pass the routes array as the first argument. You can chain feature functions: withComponentInputBinding() to map route params to component inputs, withViewTransitions() for smooth page transitions, withDebugTracing() for development logging, and withHashLocation() for static hosting. This replaces the old RouterModule.forRoot(routes) used in NgModule-based apps.

interviewQuestions.q: What is lazy loading and how do you implement it with standalone components?
interviewQuestions.a: Lazy loading defers downloading a component's JavaScript until the user first navigates to its route. With standalone components, replace component: MyComponent with loadComponent: () => import('./my.component').then(m => m.MyComponent). Angular's bundler creates a separate JavaScript chunk for that component. For an entire feature area, use loadChildren: () => import('./feature/feature.routes').then(m => m.featureRoutes) to bundle all the feature's components together.

interviewQuestions.q: What does withComponentInputBinding() do and how do you use route parameters with it?
interviewQuestions.a: withComponentInputBinding() (passed to provideRouter()) makes Angular automatically bind route parameters, query parameters, and route data to component inputs by name. With it enabled, a route defined as { path: 'user/:id' } will set the component's input named 'id' to the value from the URL. Declare it as id = input.required<string>() in the component class. Without this feature, you would need to inject ActivatedRoute and subscribe to paramMap manually.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between RouterLink and a plain <a href>?
interviewQuestions.a: A plain <a href> triggers a full browser reload — the page is fetched from the server, the JavaScript re-executes, all Angular services are destroyed and recreated. RouterLink uses Angular's router to navigate purely on the client — no server request, no reload, all state preserved. RouterLink also supports the routerLinkActive directive for CSS class toggling, array-form paths, and query params. Always use RouterLink in Angular templates.

interviewQuestions.q: What are nested routes and what problem do they solve?
interviewQuestions.a: Nested (child) routes define a hierarchy where a parent component provides a persistent layout and a <router-outlet> that child routes render into. This solves the layout duplication problem — the sidebar, header, and navigation do not have to be re-rendered or re-mounted on every page navigation. Only the content area (inside the outlet) swaps. The parent route is configured with a children array; the parent component must have its own <router-outlet> in its template.

interviewQuestions.q: What is a route guard and how do you create a functional guard?
interviewQuestions.a: A route guard is a function that runs before a route activates and returns true (allow), false (deny), or a UrlTree (redirect). Create a functional guard by exporting a CanActivateFn: export const authGuard: CanActivateFn = (route, state) => { const auth = inject(AuthService); return auth.isLoggedIn() || router.createUrlTree(['/login']); }. Add it to the route with canActivate: [authGuard]. inject() works inside functional guards because Angular runs them in an injection context.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between router.navigate() and router.navigateByUrl()?
interviewQuestions.a: router.navigate(['/path', param]) takes an array of path segments and an options object — it builds the URL for you and is useful for dynamic segments. router.navigateByUrl('/path/42') takes a pre-built URL string and is simpler but less flexible. Both accept options like { replaceUrl: true } (replaces history instead of pushing) and { queryParams: {...} }. navigate() is generally preferred because the array form is more composable and avoids string concatenation bugs.

interviewQuestions.q: When would you use the data property on a route and how do you read it?
interviewQuestions.a: The data property attaches static metadata to a route: { path: 'admin', data: { breadcrumb: 'Admin', roles: ['admin'] } }. Common uses: breadcrumb labels, required roles, feature flags, page titles. With withComponentInputBinding(), route data is automatically bound to component inputs by matching key names. Without it, inject ActivatedRoute and read route.snapshot.data['breadcrumb'] or subscribe to route.data observable for reactive updates.

interviewQuestions.q: How does the wildcard route work and why must it be last?
interviewQuestions.a: The wildcard route { path: '**', ... } matches any URL that didn't match any previous route — it is the 404 catch-all. Angular evaluates routes from top to bottom and uses the first match. If the wildcard were placed first, it would match every URL and no other route would ever activate. Placing it last ensures all specific routes are checked first and only unmatched URLs reach the wildcard.

interviewQuestions.q: What is the difference between pathMatch: 'full' and pathMatch: 'prefix'?
interviewQuestions.a: pathMatch: 'prefix' (the default) matches if the route's path matches the beginning of the URL. pathMatch: 'full' only matches if the route's path matches the entire URL. For redirect routes and the empty path '', 'full' is almost always required. Without it, { path: '', redirectTo: '/home' } with prefix matching would match EVERY URL (since every URL starts with ''), causing an infinite redirect loop. Always use pathMatch: 'full' on empty-path redirects.
